story:
The family will be there at 10:30. Helen is brushing Emily’s hair hard because Emily has the agency appointment, and I’m taking Sofia—and Travis—to the park to meet the Jacksons. Travis barrels down the stairs in his cape, a midair leap that’s both reckless and precise. He lands clean, declares he’s a good guy, and protests changing out of the costume. I get him into shorts and a shirt anyway. They’ll see he’s a good guy when they spend time with him.

At the park I’ve chosen for a quiet Tuesday morning, the stream glints through the trees, and retirees fill the tennis courts. I want calm, space, time. Today is for Travis. He and Sofia bicker in the back seat over nothing and everything. I set us up early—jungle gym, swings, a playground routine to bleed the nerves. I ask Travis if he’s a good guy, a tough guy. He nods, small and brave.

The Jacksons pull in—maroon CR-V, somehow obvious. Philip kneels first, speaks to Travis first. Good. Melanie follows with the same warmth. They race him toward the courts, and I hang back, giving them room. “Sofia, let’s check the stream,” I call, shepherding her away to protect his moment.

Sofia finds a sparkling-blue damselfly. Her white shoes sink into mud; her favorite dress goes dark at the hem. They are only clothes. The damselfly shoots back to the water and I remind her: out of the stream. My phone vibrates. It’s John with two execs. I ask Melanie if she can keep an eye on Sofia a minute; she nods. I step away but near enough to keep the group within reach.

On the call they thank me for team restructuring and training the guy who took the job I turned down. I ask plainly for the raise. They say yes—more than I asked—and they want to talk about more responsibility. I’m grateful. I’m also aware I’m in a park, on PTO, while a boy I love is meeting the people who might become his parents.

I hang up. Philip is cupping his hands, calling “Travis!” Melanie is scanning the trees, smaller somehow. Sofia streaks toward me in tears, dirt on her face: they can’t find him. I ask the questions that matter. Was he mad? Were you playing? Did someone yell? “The man yelled at him.” So he’s upset.

I jog to the path. Melanie has 911 on her screen and clutches the phone to her chest, ashamed. I tell them I know where he’ll be. There’s a fallen tree that looks like a portal, a narrow path to the stream. Philip looks to me for permission. I give him the directions and stay with Sofia, because I should have stayed with her all along. Travis doesn’t want to be found by strangers when he’s flooded. He also needs to be found by them.

We regroup at my car. I buckle kids in. I tell Philip and Melanie it’s okay, that Travis’s imagination is as big as his heart, that the day is a lot to ask of any child. Melanie swallows back tears. She didn’t call the authorities. I thank her. I say they can call, if they want.

After dinner, outside, I reread my list of reasons for the raise. Travis curls into my lap, smelling of dirt and sun. “I’m sorry,” I whisper into his hair.

“They think I’m the bad guy,” he says, small.

“No,” I want to say. You’re the hero who jumped without a landing plan. You just need someone who knows where you’ll run when it all gets too big.
